1: wĕhārāʿāḇ kāḇēḏ bāʾāreṣ — "The famine was heavy (kāḇēḏ) in the land." Kāḇēḏ (H3515, heavy/severe/weighty) — the same root as kāḇôḏ (glory, the weightiness of God's presence). In narrative usage kāḇēḏ describes the severity of plagues, famines, and hardships: the Egyptian slavery is "heavy" in Exodus 1:14; Pharaoh's heart is "heavy" (Hiphil, hardened) in Exodus 7:14. The famine's kāḇēḏ is what drives every subsequent action in Genesis 43–45.
2: wayhî kaʾăšer killû lĕʾĕkōl ʾet-hašŝeḇer — "When they had finished eating all the grain they had brought." Killû (Piel of kālāh, H3615, to finish/consume/complete) — the exhaustion of provisions creates narrative compulsion; the brothers must return. The same kālāh root is used for the completion of creation (Genesis 2:1-2) and for the covenant formulas "as of this day" — the verb of finality that drives action.
3: hēʿēḏ hēʿîḏ ḇānû hāʾîš lēʾmōr lōʾ-tirʾû pānay — "The man solemnly warned us, 'You shall not see my face.'" The infinitive absolute + finite verb construction hēʿēḏ hēʿîḏ (Hiphil infinitive absolute + Hiphil perfect of ʿûḏ, H5749, to testify/warn) — "he warned with a warning" — the emphatic doubling that signals an irrevocable, solemn declaration. The same construction underlies "dying you will die" (Genesis 2:17: môṯ tāmûṯ) and "being cut off he will be cut off" for covenant violations. Joseph's injunction is given the force of oath-like finality.
4: ʾim-yešĕḵā mĕšalēaḥ ʾet-ʾāḥînû ʾittānû nērĕḏāh wĕnišbĕrāh-llĕḵā ʾōḵel — "If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you." Mĕšalēaḥ (Piel participle of šālaḥ, H7971, to send/release) — the sending-verb that has governed the narrative: Jacob sent Joseph (Genesis 37:13-14), God will declare he sent Joseph (Genesis 45:5,8). Here Judah uses the participle condition: "if you are sending." The entire chapter turns on this sending-authorization.
5: wĕʾim-ʾênĕḵā mĕšalēaḥ lōʾ nērēḏ — "But if you will not send him, we will not go down." The parallel negative condition is blunt — there is no diplomatic softening. The brothers have accepted Benjamin's necessity as non-negotiable. The statement reverses the earlier dynamic of Genesis 42 where Jacob had authority; now Judah speaks with conditional authority over the family's course.
6: wayyōʾmer yiśrāʾēl lāmmāh harēʾōtem lî lĕhaggîḏ lāʾîš ha-ʿôḏ lāḵem ʾāḥ — "Israel said, 'Why did you bring this trouble on me by telling the man you had another brother?'" Harēʾōtem lî (you did evil to me — Hiphil of rāʿaʿ, H7489, to do evil/harm) — Jacob uses the evil-verb (ra) for his sons' disclosure, ironically: the brothers' honest answer to Joseph's interrogation (Genesis 42:13) is what Jacob calls "evil." The narrative irony is that Jacob's protective concealment of Benjamin has been the source of the crisis.
7: wayyōʾmĕrû šāʾôl šāʾal hāʾîš lānû wĕlĕmôladtēnû — "They replied, 'The man questioned us closely (šāʾôl šāʾal) about ourselves and our family.'" The infinitive absolute + perfect again — šāʾôl šāʾal (H7592, to ask) — "he asked with asking/he questioned intensely." The brothers defend their disclosure: they were interrogated, not volunteering. The emphasis distinguishes innocent compliance from treachery.
8: šĕlaḥ hannáʿar ʾittî wĕnāqûmāh wĕnēlēḵāh wĕniḥyeh wĕlōʾ nāmût — "Send the boy with me and we will arise and go, that we may live and not die." Judah's plea is structured around the life/death polarity: wĕniḥyeh (we shall live, from ḥāyāh, H2421) vs. wĕlōʾ nāmût (we shall not die, from mût, H4191). This same polarity governs the entire Joseph cycle: the brothers' survival is the stated divine purpose Joseph will articulate in Genesis 45:5 (lĕmiḥyāh, to keep alive). Life is the narrative's telos.
9: ʾānōḵî eʿĕrĕbennû miyyāḏî tĕḇaqšennû — "I myself will be surety for him; from my hand you shall require him." ʾĒraḇ (H6148, to become surety/pledge, to give security for another) — the same root as ʿēreḇôn (pledge, Genesis 38:17-20) that Judah gave Tamar. There Judah treated a pledge as disposable; here he pledges himself with his own life. Judah's transformation from the man who traded Joseph for profit (Genesis 37:26-27) and failed his Tamar-obligation (Genesis 38:11) to the man who voluntarily becomes surety for Benjamin is the moral arc the narrative has been building. The Hiphil tĕḇaqšennû (you shall require him) — the demanding-back formula of covenant surety.
10: kî lûlēʾ hiṯmahmāhnû kî-ʿattāh šaḇnû zeh paʿămāyim — "For if we had not delayed, by now we could have gone and returned twice." Hiṯmahmāhnû (Hithpael of māhah, H4102, to linger/delay/hesitate) — the reflexive hesitation-verb; Lot is urged not to linger (hiṯmahmēah) in Sodom (Genesis 19:16). Judah's acknowledgment of the family's delay frames the urgency: each day of delay costs lives. The pragmatic argument follows the covenantal one (v9).
11: qĕḥû miznĕḇat hāʾāreṣ biḵlêḵem ûhôrîḏû lāʾîš minhāh — "Take some of the best products of the land in your bags, and carry down to the man a gift (minhāh)." Minhāh (H4503, gift/tribute/offering — also the technical term for the grain offering in Leviticus) — the same word Jacob used when sending gifts to appease Esau (Genesis 32:14,19; Genesis 33:10). The gift-list that follows — balm, honey, spices, myrrh, pistachios, almonds — is virtually identical to the cargo of the Ishmaelite traders who carried Joseph to Egypt (Genesis 37:25). Jacob sends the produce of Canaan to Egypt by the same goods-channel that his other son traveled. The narrative parallelism is pointed.
12: wĕḵesef mišneh qĕḥû ḇĕyedḵem — "Take double the silver in your hand." Mišneh (H4932, double/second) — the double-silver instruction accounts for the possibility that the first return of silver was accidental; doubling it removes any grounds for accusation. The root šānāh (to repeat/double) governs the Joseph narrative structurally: Joseph's double dreams (Genesis 37:5-10); Pharaoh's double dreams (Genesis 41:1-8); the double silver returned in sacks (Genesis 42:25-35) — divine doubling as the signature of certainty (Genesis 41:32).
13: wĕʾet-ʾăḥîḵem qāḥû wĕqûmû šûḇû ʾel-hāʾîš — "Take your brother also, and arise, go back to the man." Jacob's three imperatives (qāḥû… qûmû… šûḇû — take, arise, return) signal a final authorization after all his resistance. The word šûḇû (return, H7725) — the return-journey verb that bookends the Egypt episodes. Jacob has yielded to necessity; his next speech (v14) reframes the surrender as faith.
14: wĕʾănî kaʾăšer šāḵōltî šāḵāltî — "And as for me, if I am bereaved, I am bereaved." Šāḵōltî šāḵāltî (Qal infinitive absolute + perfect of šāḵal, H7921, to be bereaved of children, to lose offspring) — the infinitive absolute used for resigned finality: "if I am going to lose children, I will lose them." The same verb describes Rachel "weeping for her children" in Jeremiah 31:15 (the mĕšakkēlāh, the one bereaved of children, cited in Matthew 2:18). Jacob's acceptance formula is not passive fatalism but a release of control: he cannot prevent loss, so he commends his sons to El Shaddai and lets go.
15: wayyiqqĕḥû hāʾănāšîm ʾet-hamminhāh hazzōʾt ûmišneh-kesef lāqĕḥû bĕyāḏām wĕʾet-binyāmîn — "The men took this gift, and double silver in their hand, and Benjamin." The three-item preparation — gift, double silver, Benjamin — executes Jacob's triple command (v13: take, arise, return). The narrative compression ("they took and went down") moves swiftly to Egypt; the lingering has been in Canaan; the action unfolds in Egypt.
16: wayyarʾ yôsēp ʾittām ʾet-binyāmîn wayyōʾmer laʾăšer ʿal-bêtô hāḇēʾ ʾet-hāʾănāšîm habbāytāh ûṭĕḇōaḥ ṭeḇaḥ — "Joseph saw Benjamin with them and said to his household steward, 'Bring the men into the house, and slaughter an animal and prepare it.'" Ṭĕḇōaḥ ṭeḇaḥ (slaughter a slaughter — Qal infinitive absolute + imperative of ṭāḇaḥ, H2873) — the emphatic slaughtering command; ṭāḇaḥ is the root of ṭabbāḥ (slaughterer/bodyguard), the title of Potiphar and his office (Genesis 39:1). The royal slaughter for a feast signals immediate, lavish hospitality — the highest honor code of the ancient world. Joseph's first act on seeing Benjamin is to prepare a banquet.
17: wayyaʿaś hāʾîš kaʾăšer ʾāmar yôsēp wayyāḇēʾ hāʾîš ʾet-hāʾănāšîm bêtāh yôsēp — "The man did as Joseph said, and brought the men into Joseph's house." The steward executes the command without delay — the household operates under Joseph's complete authority. The men are brought "into Joseph's house" — the same household that Potiphar once entrusted entirely to Joseph (Genesis 39:4-5), now restored as Joseph's own domain.
18: wayyîrĕʾû hāʾănāšîm kî hûḇĕʾû bêt yôsēp wayyōʾmĕrû ʿal-dĕḇar hakkesef — "The men were afraid because they had been brought into Joseph's house, and they said, 'It is because of the silver.'" Wayyîrĕʾû (they feared, H3372) — their fear is reasonable: in their cultural framework, being brought to an official's private house meant accusation, not hospitality. The returned silver (Genesis 42:25-35) has made them guilty-feeling, and the private invitation confirms their anxiety. Their interior speech anticipates slavery and death — the maximum penalty they fear.
19: wayyiggĕšû ʾel-hāʾîš ʾăšer ʿal-bêt yôsēp wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾēlāyw petaḥ habbāyit — "They came near to the man who was over Joseph's household and spoke with him at the entrance of the house." Wayyiggĕšû (they drew near, Niphal of nāgaš, H5066) — the approach for communication or supplication; Judah will use the same approach before Joseph in 44:18 for his climactic plea. The entrance (petaḥ, H6607) is the threshold space where status negotiations occur — Lot met the angels at the entrance of Sodom (Genesis 19:1).
20: wayyōʾmĕrû bî ʾăḏōnî yārōḏ yāraḏnû ḇattĕḥillāh lišbor-ʾōḵel — "They said, 'Please, my lord, we came down the first time to buy food.'" Bî ʾăḏōnî (please, my lord — literally "by/through me, my lord") — the formal petition particle; the same phrase used in Abraham's bargaining with God (Genesis 18:27,30,32). The brothers are positioning themselves as supplicants before authority, echoing covenant-petition language.
21: wayhî kî-bānû ʾel-hammālôn wannipĕtĕḥāh ʾet-ʾamtĕḥōtênû wĕhinnēh kesef-ʾîš bĕpî ʾamtaḥtô — "When we came to the lodging place we opened our sacks, and behold, each man's silver was in the mouth of his sack, full weight." The wĕhinnēh (behold!) marks the brothers' subjective shock — the silver was an astonishing, unexplainable discovery. Their explanation of it to the steward establishes their innocence: they are not hiding it but reporting it. The returned silver is framed as supernatural, not theft.
22: wĕḵesef ʾaḥēr hôraḏnû bĕyāḏênû lišbor-ʾōḵel lōʾ yāḏaʿnû mî-śām kaspēnû bĕʾamtĕḥōtênû — "And we have brought additional silver with us to buy food; we don't know who put our silver in our sacks." The double silver returns as evidence of good faith: they have the original silver plus more. Their disclaimer of knowledge — "we do not know who put it" — is honest but also ironic: the reader knows Joseph ordered it (Genesis 42:25). The gap between narrator-knowledge and character-knowledge creates the dramatic tension.
23: wayyōʾmer šālôm lāḵem ʾal-tîrāʾû ʾĕlōhêḵem wēʾlōhê ʾăḇîḵem nātan lāḵem maṭmôn bĕʾamtĕḥōtêḵem — "He said, 'Peace to you, do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure in your sacks.'" The steward's words are theologically remarkable: an Egyptian household official attributes the returned silver to "your God and the God of your father" — using the exact covenant-identification formula. Whether he has been briefed by Joseph (likely) or speaks from genuine theological awareness, the words are the narrator's device: God's providence is being confessed through an Egyptian mouth, before the revelation of Genesis 45:5-8. Šālôm (H7965, peace/wholeness/well-being) — the covenant-peace word; it will resonate when the brothers eventually return to their father carrying news of Joseph's shalom (Genesis 45:9).
24: wayyāḇēʾ hāʾîš ʾet-hāʾănāšîm bêtāh yôsēp wayyittēn-māyim wayyirḥăṣû raglêhem — "The man brought the men into Joseph's house and gave them water, and they washed their feet." Foot-washing for guests was the standard hospitality gesture of the ancient world (cf. Abraham's washing the feet of the three visitors in Genesis 18:4; Lot in Genesis 19:2). The sequence — water, foot-washing, fodder for the donkeys, table prepared — follows the full ANE hospitality protocol. The brothers are being received as honored guests by the very man who they fear intends to enslave them.
25: wayyāḵînû ʾet-hamminhāh ʿaḏ-ḇôʾ yôsēp ḇaṣṣohŏrāyim kî šāmĕʿû kî-šām yōʾḵĕlû leḥem — "They prepared the gift for Joseph's arrival at noon, for they heard that they were to eat a meal there." Ṣohŏrāyim (H6672, noon/midday) — the noon hour is a time of heat, rest, and formal meals in the ancient Near East. The brothers' preparation of the gift while waiting reflects ANE gift-protocol: the tribute is prepared and presented at the moment of meeting, not left casually. The verb hēkîn (prepared, Hiphil of kûn, H3559, to establish/prepare/make ready) — the same word for divine preparation throughout the Psalms (Psalms 23:5: "you prepare a table before me").
26: wayyāḇōʾ yôsēp habbāytāh wayyāḇîʾû lô ʾet-hamminhāh ʾăšer bĕyāḏām habbāytāh wayyiššĕtaḥăwû lô ʾarṣāh — "Joseph came into the house, and they brought the gift into the house to him and bowed down (wayyiššĕtaḥăwû) before him to the ground." Hištaḥăwāh (Hithpael of šāḥāh, H7812, to bow down/prostrate oneself) — the full prostration; the fulfillment of Joseph's first dream: "your sheaves bowed down to my sheaf" (Genesis 37:7, tishtaḥăwaynāh). The brothers enact Joseph's dream without knowing it. This is the third occurrence: first in Genesis 42:6 (ten brothers bow), again in Genesis 42:9 (Joseph remembered the dreams), now with Benjamin present completing the eleven.
27: wayyišʾal lāhem lĕšālôm wayyōʾmer hăšālôm ʾăḇîḵem hazāqēn ʾăšer ʾămartem ha-ʿôḏĕnnû ḥay — "He asked about their welfare and said, 'Is your father well, the old man you spoke of? Is he still alive?'" Joseph's first question is about his father; šālôm (peace/welfare) is asked twice — for the brothers collectively, then specifically for the father. The father-question is the emotional center: Joseph has not seen Jacob for over twenty years. The phrase ha-ʿôḏĕnnû ḥay (is he still alive) — the same question Joseph will ask with tears after the revelation (Genesis 45:3).
28: wayyōʾmĕrû šālôm lĕʿaḇdĕḵā lĕʾāḇînû ʿôḏennû ḥay wayyiqĕḏû wayyiššĕtaḥăwû — "They said, 'Your servant our father is well; he is still alive.' And they bowed and prostrated themselves." A second prostration — the brothers bow again. Their answer uses the covenant-speech formula: "your servant our father" — positioning Jacob as a subordinate of Joseph even as they report his health. The double-bow (v26 and v28) underscores the dream-fulfillment. The formula ʿôḏennû ḥay (he is still alive) — the life-affirmation that Joseph desperately needed to hear.
29: wayyiśśāʾ ʿênāyw wayyarʾ ʾet-binyāmîn ʾāḥîw ben-ʾimmô wayyōʾmer ha-zeh ʾăḥîḵem haqqāṭān… ʾĕlōhîm yāḥunnĕḵā ḇĕnî — "He looked up and saw his brother Benjamin, his mother's son… and said, 'God be gracious to you, my son.'" Ben-ʾimmô (son of his mother) — Benjamin alone shares Joseph's mother Rachel; the identification is the deepest possible kinship designation. The blessing ʾĕlōhîm yāḥunnĕḵā (God be gracious to you) — Qal imperfect jussive of ḥānan (H2603, to be gracious, show favor, act with covenant-grace) — Joseph blesses his full-brother with the same grace-vocabulary that defines God's covenant relationship. The word ḇĕnî (my son) from a man only slightly older than Benjamin is an emotional overflow, not a literal claim.
30: wayyĕmahēr yôsēp kî-niḵmĕrû raḥămāyw ʾel-ʾāḥîw wayyĕḇaqqēš liḇkôṯ — "Joseph hurried out, for his compassion grew warm (niḵmĕrû raḥămāyw) for his brother, and he sought to weep." Two remarkable verbs: kāmar (H3648, Niphal: to grow warm/be deeply stirred, to be kindled with emotion — used for the woman whose maternal compassion "yearned" for her child in 1 Kings 3:26: wayyikhmĕrû raḥămeyhā) + raḥămîm (H7356, deep mercies/compassion — the plural of reḥem, womb, the seat of maternal-covenantal love). Together these two words describe the most intense, physiologically-located emotional response in biblical Hebrew: Joseph's body responds to Benjamin as a womb responds to its child. The narrator is telling us that Joseph's love for his brother is of the same quality as the divine raḥămîm — God's womb-love for Israel (Isaiah 49:15).
31: wayyirḥaṣ pānāyw wayyēṣēʾ wayyiṯʾappaq wayyōʾmer śîmû leḥem — "He washed his face and came out, and controlling himself (wayyiṯʾappaq) said, 'Serve the meal.'" Wayyiṯʾappaq (Hithpael of ʾāpaq, H662, to restrain/control oneself) — the same verb used for Joseph's self-restraint breaking in Genesis 45:1 (lōʾ-yāḵōl lĕhiṯʾappēq, could no longer restrain himself). Here in 43:31 he succeeds in containing himself; in 45:1 the dam breaks. The Hithpael reflexive — he controls himself against himself — captures the enormous internal effort of suppression. Face-washing restores his composure for public presentation.
32: wayyāśîmû lô lĕḇaddô wĕlāhem lĕḇaddām wĕlammṣirîm hāʾōḵlîm ʾittô lĕḇaddām kî lōʾ yûḵlûn hammiṣrîm lĕʾĕḵōl ʾet-hāʿiḇrîm leḥem kî-tôʿēḇāh hîʾ lĕmiṣrāyim — "They served him by himself, and them by themselves, and the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, because the Egyptians could not eat bread with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination (tôʿēḇāh) to Egypt." Tôʿēḇāh (H8441, abomination/detestable thing) — the same strong ritual-purity term used throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy for Israel's covenant prohibitions (Deuteronomy 7:25-26; Leviticus 18:22). Here it appears from the Egyptian side: sharing a table with Hebrews is an abomination to Egyptians. The narrator presents this without condemnation — it is a simple cultural-religious fact that shapes the logistics. The three-table arrangement (Joseph, Egyptians, Hebrews) captures the social stratification: Joseph alone at the apex, the Egyptians separate, the brothers separate.
33: wayyēšĕḇû lĕpānāyw habĕḵōr kĕḇeḵōrātô wĕhaṣṣāʿîr kiṣĕʿîrātô wayyitĕmĕhû hāʾănāšîm ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû — "They were seated before him — the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth — and the men looked at one another in amazement (wayyitĕmĕhû)." Wayyitĕmĕhû (Hithpael of tāmah, H8539, to be astonished/stupefied) — the brothers are speechless with amazement. The seating by birth-order — when they have never told Joseph their ages — is an inexplicable miracle to them. It is the first crack in the naturalistic explanation of events: something beyond coincidence is ordering their affairs. The birth-order seating also directly reverses the birth-order violation that started the family's crisis: Jacob's favoring of Joseph over older brothers.
34: wayyiśśāʾ mǎśʾōt mēʾittô ʾălêhem waṯṯēreḇ maśʾat binyāmîn mimmǎśʾōt kullām ḥāmēš yāḏôt — "Portions were sent to them from his table, and Benjamin's portion was five times as much as anyone else's." Ḥāmēš yāḏôt (five hands/portions) — the number five is the royal Egyptian number throughout the Joseph cycle: five changes of garments for Benjamin (Genesis 45:22), five years of famine remaining (Genesis 45:6), one-fifth taken during the seven good years (Genesis 41:34). The "five portions" for Benjamin parallel the five-fold honor — Benjamin receives royal-scale provision before the brothers know his provider. The measure of provision is the measure of love Joseph cannot yet openly express.
